system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 6: Jazz Standard Study #5: Sweet Georgia Brown
Pages 87-101 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Sweet Georgia Brown is a jazz standard that packs a couple of valu-
able jazz harmony lessons into one song, and I’m excited to go over
them with you. Like all of these standards, you can learn so much from
studying this one and applying some of these new and familiar con-
cepts to others. First things first.

Sweet Georgia Brown Bie

Sweet Georgia Brown was written in 1925 by Ben Bernie and Maceo Pinkard.
Bernie first recorded it in the same year with his Hotel Roosevelt Orchestra. The
song was later adopted as the theme song for the Harlem Globetrotters basket-
ball team in 1952. This song is commonly played in concert Ab major.

One thing | love about this tune is how it has been played throughout
different eras of jazz history. Its Dixie Land (early jazz) compositional
arrangement has been morphed to fit different styles.

Gypsy jazz players have adopted this tune as it fits perfectly with
style, but bebop players and beyond have used this tune as an impro-
visational vehicle.

When learning to play this song (or any song), it’s important to know
its roots, and the time it was coming from. Be sure to listen to the orig-
inal recording by Ben Bernie’s orchestra in the Suggested Listening
section. Hearing its original arrangement and general feeling will be
quite insightful as you think about how you can improvise over it in your
own style.


>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Ben Bernie and his Orchestra from “Sweet Georgia
Brown and Other Hot Numbers” 1923-1929

Benny Goodman from “Benny Rides Again” 2012

Dizzy Gillespie from “The Legend Collection” 2012

Ella Fitzgerald from “A Perfect Match- Ella and
Basie” 1979

Give this song a bunch of listens and be sure to go out and research
some of your own. As always, the next several pages will include the
chord map, guide tone chart, and chords analysis. Then I'll dive into
the harmony with you so you can understand it better.

Descending Chromatic Approach to VI7 ii-V-I

Let’s go over the color coding just so we are on the same page.

Blue = Parent key center
Green = A brief key center change to the VI

Red = The relative minor key
‘Yellow = A chromatic descending section

Let’s take a look at the harmony of Sweet Georgia Brown and discern
what is happening here.

The first thing to point out is that this song does not start on the |
chord. Most of the jazz standards we have studied so far start on the
| chord making the key center quite obvious, except Autumn Leaves,
which started on the ii of the relative major.

Sweet Georgia Brown starts on the VI7. |t wouldn’t seem like such a
surprising way to start if it was a minor 7 chord (meaning it would be
the relative minor). However, the vi minor is converted into a VI domi-
nant.

So the first 4 bars hang out on the VI7, and in bars 5-8 it moves quite
expectedly through the cycle of 4ths to the ii chord. Except once again
the ii chord is made into a dominant 7 chord (II7).

Now, if you’re the kind of person that has a problem with breaking the
rules, jazz may not be the most comforting music for you to study.
Why is it possible to change the chord quality of chords within the
diatonic series?

Because why not. So far in our studies, we have observed that a minor
vi chord can be turned into a dominant VI7 chord in the context of a
I-VI-ii-V or even a iii-VI-ii-V. ’ve explained that it creates more tension
and release, therefore enhancing the voice leading movement of the
chord progression. We’ve seen a few other cases of this as well but
usually, have justified the changing of chord qualities as relating to a
different key center.

But in this case, the VI7 and the II7 are clearly relating to the parent
key center (as highlighted in blue). Don’t get too hung up on this, just
know that it is entirely normal and acceptable to turn minor chords in
the diatonic series into dominant 7“ chords.


Bars 9-12 hang on the V chord which is always dominant. So really,
bars 1-13 are just a long cycle of dominant 7* chords resolving to the |
chord.

Let’s take a quick look at the green section I’ve made a point to high-
light.

Gun’ Cc’

- ti-V of VI7

This part has a fairly simple explanation. This is a quick turnaround
back to the VI7. We are briefly entering the key of F major, although as
we know, it resolves to an F7 instead.

Let’s take a look at the next part of interest:

BY” BY” C709

Starting in the second half of bar 24 we begin to transition into the
relative minor key. What's the relative minor to Ab major? F minor.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 96 |



| have these chords labeled as ii-V of vi and so on because | think this
song is easiest understood relating back to the parent key. Still, it’s
important to recognize this is the relative minor we are dealing with.

What’s relieving about Sweet Georgia Brown is like Autumn Leaves,
you can basically sum it up as revolving around the major and relative
minor keys. Sure, there are some tiny exceptions here and there if we
choose to think of them differently, but generally, this song isn’t overly
complicated.

Here’s the last part of interest:

Na G7 6’ F7 Bn? 7 APwas”

* Descending Chromatic Approach to VI7_ ii-V-I-------------------------

Don't let this section throw you either. Understand we are just dealing
with a series of chromatically descending dominant 7“ chords that are
targeting the VI7 chord. From there the VI7 logically concludes to a ii-
V-| to the parent tonic.

You can simplify your understanding of this section by noting that it
starts on the I7 and is targeting the VI7. You can think of bars 29-32
as a “backdoor” |7-VI7-ii-V-I.

Gm On a en

Descending Chromatic Approach to VI7_ ii-V-I - - - - ---------------------

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



IMPROV LESSON
Navigating Cycling Dorinent 7” Chords

| would say the biggest takeaway from Sweet Georgia Brown is it is a
fantastic study of cycling dominant 7% chords in 4ths.

But how do we improvise over them? They can be especially challeng-
ing to approach considering that each chord lasts for 4 bars each. In
my opinion, that’s the hardest part about it. Some disagree and would
invite less harmonic movement, but for me, | find it harder to create
melodies with fewer chords to play over.

When it comes down to it, becoming a great jazz improviser is about
learning jazz language by mimicking great jazz musicians and under-
standing the lingo. However, | think it is helpful also to have some basic
tools to work with.

To approach these cycling dominant 7 chords | want to combine both
by providing exercises as well as some licks to practice. But first, let’s
start with a scale that can be helpful over dominant 7* chords.

THE MIXOLYDIAN SCALE

Recorded 15ma

=F:

L
Y

hue

ao

The Mixolydian scale is the 5th mode of the major scale. |’ll talk more
about modes in the next jazz standard study, so don’t worry if you
don’t quite understand what that means.

The easiest way to think of a Mixolydian scale is a major scale with a
b7. Or you can think of it as starting and ending a major scale on its
5* tone.

Formula: 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7


Because of the b7, this scale is perfect for using as a tool to find note
choices over a dominant 7. Yes, | said “note choices” on purpose. The
best way to think about the use of scales is “pitch collections.” They
aren’t notes that are meant to be played in order, nor are they to be
used solely to improvise. They are simply pitch collections which map
out the note choices you can use to make melodies over a particular
chord. So automatically you can use the Mixolydian scale to improvise
over these chords.

Here’s an exercise | want you to try. | have taken the first 12 bars of
Sweet Georgia Brown and composed a simple pattern using the Mix-
olydian scale. Play through it for yourself so you can get a better idea
of how this works.

Note that each bar starts on a different chord tone of the given chord.
The first and last lines begin with quarter note chord tones moving in
ascending order: R-3'*-5*-b7. The second line descends in this order:
R-b7-5°%-3°¢.

There are undoubtedly different patterns or rhythms you could use.
This is just one | utilized, and | encourage you to practice this one, get
it under your chops, and then create one of your own.

Sometimes simple exercises like these can help you explore your note
choices and even move in a more musical direction. Ultimately, you
don’t want to improvise like this, but it’s a great starting point to map
out your options.

Now let’s shake off the methodical exercise approach and move to a
more musical one. This time instead of looking at the first 12 bars of
Sweet Georgia Brown as a whole, we are going to take a look at each
dominant 7° chord individually.

I’m going to list out a series of licks for you to play over these dominant
7” chords. Learning licks from your favorites jazz musicians, finding
them in books, and even creating your own are really great practices.
In past improv lessons we have looked at composing music on a macro
level, but licks are great for breaking things down to a micro level.

I’m a big believer in less is more, so | want you to stick to practicing
these three licks for now. But the great thing about any short musical
phrase like this is you can take it to other keys.

| always suggest taking things through all 12 keys. It’s a powerful
practice that not only helps you internalize musical information, but it
also enables you to become stronger in keys you may be weaker at.
Make it your goal to practice one of these licks through all 12 keys, or
create your own and do the same thing.

Sweet Georgia Brown is an excellent jazz standard to study, and | hope
you see why | picked it as one of the ten. Feel free to reference back to
this chapter and use the different resources | have provided.

But for now, let’s move on to our next jazz standard study: Se What.
